title: Tatum Project Kickoff — Spec Skills + Architecture Discussion

speaker: Reed
content:
I'm starting this project called Tatum which is a sexually activity tracking app. This is an expo app for a Build with Tori client and I want to use regenerative software patterns (Debrief/Phoenix Architecture) to build it.

I've included the two main documents we sent our client about what we would build. These have HTML documents for specific designs and such.

speaker: Reed
content: P.S. please read Documents/Claude/Projects/Tatum/regenerative-expo-architecture.md for how the Debrief/Phoenix architecture should be implemented for this Expo app.

summary: Read three project documents: regenerative-expo-architecture.md (6-layer Phoenix Architecture for Expo), tatum-moodboard.html (full design system mockup with colors, typography, interactive components), and tatum-concept-1.pdf (5-page client concept document covering three pillars, revenue model, target audience)

content: Reviewed all three documents and saved project context to auto-memory. Presented summary of Tatum (three pillars: Tracker, Safe Space, Whisper), the regenerative architecture (6 layers, specs survive, src/ is ephemeral), and the design system (Terra/Fig/Mauve/Blush palette, Playfair Display + DM Sans). Asked Reed what to tackle first.

speaker: Reed
content: [Selected: Write the spec skills — Create the .claude/skills/ layer first — data model skill, feature skills for each pillar, design system skill — before any code]

speaker: Reed
content: [Clarified: Project repo is at ~/Developer/skylight-social/tatum, empty git repo. Yes, include a routing skill.]

summary: Created 12 SKILL.md files in .claude/skills/: data-model (9 entities — Encounter, ActivityEmoji, Partner, PrivateNote, DesireEntry, WhisperMessage, WhisperTemplate, Affirmation, UserProfile — with relationships, storage strategy, computed views), design-system (full color palette, typography, component patterns, brand voice with The One Rule, animation principles), emoji-calendar (drag-drop interaction, day states, quick log flow), partner-profiles (creation, avatars, rating panel with thumbs/stars/vibes, partner stats), private-notes (encrypted journal, encryption strategy), safe-space (desire logging, pattern recognition, connection to Whisper), tatum-whisper (prompt templates, composition flow, SMS/copy delivery), routing (5-tab layout with center FAB, all routes mapped, modal patterns, navigation flows), revenue-tiers (free vs premium gating, upsell UX), stats-screen (daily/weekly free, monthly/yearly premium, chart patterns), affirmations (daily greeting, custom library), milestones (emoji and achievement milestones, celebration UX). Total: 1,504 lines of spec.

Ran verification agent to compare all skills against the concept document. Found gaps: no stats screen skill, no affirmations skill, no milestones skill, Affirmation entity missing from data model. Created the three missing skills and updated data-model with Affirmation entity.

speaker: Reed
content: I think it's worth thinking about what information maybe it's in the codebase yet. If you empathize with the Claude Code session that's going to read this stuff, what knowledge is it not going to have access to?

Identified five gaps a Claude Code session would face: (1) No CLAUDE.md entry point for the project, (2) the regenerative architecture philosophy doc isn't in the repo, (3) no tech stack decisions documented (Zustand vs Jotai, which SQLite wrapper, etc.), (4) the visual reference (moodboard HTML) isn't accessible from within the repo, (5) the 'why' behind product decisions is thin — competitive context, target audience depth, emotional positioning from the concept doc.

speaker: Reed
content:
These are all good points and they connect back to what we're trying to do with debrief: Context about the project history is copied in to the codebase from chat/cowork sessions. This gives us the 'why' it gives us more context about the visuals, etc. The project started with a conversation and that conversation is important for really understanding what you're building.

We probably don't need a CLAUDE.md though. We can have a README.md which Claude can look at when it's confused but the problem with putting information in CLAUDE.md is that it's REALLY influential over what Claude generates so you can kind of only put stuff in there that you want Claude to be thinking about EVERY single time it's working on anything.

That's why we like Skills so much and we don't have to tell Claude to use skills, it's built in to the harness. So are plugins and we actually prefer to always write plugins and put our skills inside of those plugins instead of on their own because plugins just allow for a nicer form of portability and scoping.

So I imagine our .claude folder should have just a few plugins:
1. regenerative-expo -- regenerative software pattern for Expo
2. expo -- programming guidance for Expo
3. debrief -- all of the product context we got from chat (including most of the skills you just created)

What do you think about that? Are any parts of that not making sense?

Affirmed the three-plugin breakdown: regenerative-expo (architecture pattern, portable across projects), expo (programming-level guidance, also portable), debrief (product context from conversations — where the skills we just wrote belong). Noted that CLAUDE.md is a sledgehammer — skills and plugins are scoped. Asked whether Reed had existing regenerative-expo or expo plugin structures or if we'd create from scratch.

speaker: Reed
content: Actually I don't need you to setup the claude directory anymore than you already have we just need to /debrief and that will create the debrief plugin with all this historical context and I can do everything else once I'm in Claude Code.

summary: Installed debrief plugin at .claude/plugins/debrief/ with plugin.json, hooks (sync-context.sh), transcript, 12 product skills copied from .claude/skills/, and 3 synthesis skills (project-overview, architecture-decisions, plugin-strategy).
